label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why some groups support the new law in Virginia regarding the ignition interlock devices for drunken driving offenders. First, I need to go through the text again to find the relevant information. The passage mentions that groups like Mothers Against Drunk Driving and the Washington Regional Alcohol Program are in favor of the law. They argue that Virginia still has high numbers of alcohol-related road deaths and injuries, even with existing crackdowns. They say the ignition interlock devices reduce repeat offenses. So the key point here is that these groups support the law because they believe it will help reduce the number of traffic deaths and injuries caused by drunk driving. The law makes more first-time offenders install the devices, which they think will lead to fewer repeat offenses and thus save lives. Looking at the options: A) the government can be financed to build roads – The text doesn't mention anything about road funding. The cost of the device is mentioned as a concern, but not as a source of funding for roads. So probably not A. B) some traffic deaths and injuries may be avoided – This matches with the groups' argument that the high numbers of deaths and injuries are unacceptable and that the devices reduce repeat offenses. So B seems correct. C) lower-income drivers will not afford to drink again – The text actually mentions that some lawyers argue lower-income drivers will be affected by the fees, but that's a counterpoint, not a reason for support. The support is about reducing accidents, not about affordability of drinking.
